title: EoTV vs EoE again
wikitext:
[]: EoTV vs. EoE (again)             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Thu Mar 22, 2007 2:48 pm lol split --Reichu Mr. Tines wrote: The reason I don't see the end as uplifting is the total disconnect between show and tell. Rei and Yui may talk the talk, but at the end, we don't see anyone walking the walk -- quite the opposite, in fact.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Mar 22, 2007 3:22 pm Anonymous_Evafan wrote: Dude, what do you expect? Instant results or something?

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Thu Mar 22, 2007 3:41 pm Mr. Tines wrote: Something like... Not as if it weren't something we had been led to expect:--

[]: Answering questions with questions!             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Mar 22, 2007 4:43 pm Reichu wrote: Mr. Tines wrote: Something like... Not as if it weren't something we had been led to expect:--

[]:             Quote Post by TriLink » Thu Mar 22, 2007 4:50 pm I agree with Tines regarding this matter. Yes, I know Yui/Rei say all that mumbo jumbo about finding happiness wherever you are and all that, but it seems like with the beach scene Anno's like "Hey, you know all that stuff they just said about finding happiness...TOTAL BS! AND YOU SUCKERS FELL FOR IT!". That's how I interpret those scenes anyway, which is why I find NGE depressing. Call me shallow, but it would have gone a long way for me had Anno actually taken the time to SHOW that A/S and the gang were all okay. Instead he leaves us with a bleak and demoralizing situation, yet we're still suppose to see the bigger picture and take his word that everything is alright? It's enough to make my head ASPOLDE

[]: Re: Answering questions with questions!             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Mar 22, 2007 4:56 pm Sailor Star Dust wrote: Please care to clarify? What's the way you look at it, Tines?

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Thu Mar 22, 2007 5:04 pm That's because you're a stubborn old man. They aren't ending at the same time. The Congratulations bit is in the timeframe of his chat with Adam and Lilith, which was affirmation not in the real world, the beach is after that. EoE makes the intent of EoTV clear as day . On a more important note I'm not sure how 3 lousy minutes with TWO people (one of which is off in la-la land) is enough data to draw any conclusions from

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Mar 22, 2007 5:11 pm *Scrolls back up towards Shinji pic. Jiiiiiii....* Shit. La-la land is right. trilink wrote: Instead he leaves us with a bleak and demoralizing situation, yet we're still suppose to see the bigger picture and take his word that everything is alright?

[]:             Quote Post by TriLink » Thu Mar 22, 2007 5:18 pm It's not that I disagree with it, rather, I think it's a bit of a stetch to say that's the message in NGE's case. You could literally apply that to nearly every show out there. Rui's message of finding happiness on the other hand, although disputed by the final scene, seems much more fitting to me.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Thu Mar 22, 2007 6:42 pm Anonymous_Evafan wrote: That's because you're a stubborn old man.

[]:             Quote Post by slothen » Thu Mar 22, 2007 8:44 pm Shinji wrote: I'm on a big blue ball!

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Mar 22, 2007 9:19 pm @ Big Blue Ball: And how! Speaking of spoiling oneself, I watched the latter half of Eva out of order (including the "exiciting" bits of EoE even though I didn't get that far episode wise) though I think it was mostly because I already read summaries on the damn thing online....Though I was still confused afterwards, which is why I joined these forums. AEF wrote: The Congratulations bit is in the timeframe of his chat with Adam and Lilith, which was affirmation not in the real world, the beach is after that. EoE makes the intent of EoTV clear as day.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Thu Mar 22, 2007 9:23 pm No, not literally concurrent, but still telling the same basic story overall. Saying they're radically different stories based on the endings when they don't even seem to have the same timeframe is, well I dunno just how to describe it.

[]:             Quote Post by BrikHaus » Thu Mar 22, 2007 9:32 pm Anonymous_Evafan wrote: No, not literally concurrent, but still telling the same basic story overall. Saying they're radically different stories based on the endings when they don't even seem to have the same timeframe is, well I dunno just how to describe it.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Thu Mar 22, 2007 9:58 pm It's not that, Gendo explaining Instrumentality and other little things that can't possibly happen in EoE make me think this way.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Thu Mar 22, 2007 10:18 pm Anonymous_Evafan wrote: It's not that, Gendo explaining Instrumentality and other little things that can't possibly happen in EoE make me think this way.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Thu Mar 22, 2007 10:52 pm Reichu wrote: Anonymous_Evafan wrote: It's not that, Gendo explaining Instrumentality and other little things that can't possibly happen in EoE make me think this way.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Fri Mar 23, 2007 1:13 am TriLink12 wrote: I agree with Tines regarding this matter. Yes, I know Yui/Rei say all that mumbo jumbo about finding happiness wherever you are and all that, but it seems like with the beach scene Anno's like "Hey, you know all that stuff they just said about finding happiness...TOTAL BS! AND YOU SUCKERS FELL FOR IT!". That's how I interpret those scenes anyway, which is why I find NGE depressing. Call me shallow, but it would have gone a long way for me had Anno actually taken the time to SHOW that A/S and the gang were all okay. Instead he leaves us with a bleak and demoralizing situation, yet we're still suppose to see the bigger picture and take his word that everything is alright? It's enough to make my head ASPOLDE

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Fri Mar 23, 2007 1:29 am I like the thought about the EoTV ending happening right after the Rei/Kaworu encounter in EoE. This actually explains the wild difference in mood between the two endings. It also makes sense from a psychological perspective. The hope that things will be alright exists in one's own mind. The EoTV is all about the psychology of the characters. So the 'happy' ending represents all the hope and faith in Shinji's mind that the future will be happy as long as he's alive. EoE is NGE from a realistic perspective. As others pointed out, we don't see much of the communal instrumentality in EoE. What we do see is what lead up to Instrumentality (not discussed in EoTV), and what happens afterwards. It represents the reality of staying alive in, uhhhh, this reality. And shows that while the message of being happy in reality is a nice thought (EoTV) in practice, it's not so easy when life is so hard (EoE). If you look at the two endings on a timeline, I think you'd find that EoE fills in many of the gaps left by EoTV, and vice versa. What we have is two distinctively seperate parts that collectively make a whole ending. This might also explain why the final scene gets its own title card: because everything up until that point has already been experienced in the EoTV finale. And One More Final is the 'epilogue', so to speak, of the entire series. The more I think about the two endings the more I realize how crucial they are to each other. It's as if someone tore the last several pages out of one of the best books ever written, and then pieced some of the pages together to create one ending, and some of the pages together to make the same ending, but shed in a different light. It's actually quite ingenious IMO.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Fri Mar 23, 2007 1:36 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: This might also explain why the final scene gets its own title card: because everything up until that point has already been experienced in the EoTV finale. And One More Final is the 'epilogue', so to speak, of the entire series.

Rei and Yui may talk the talk, but at the end, we don't see anyone walking the walk -- quite the opposite, in fact.
